questions.question: What are generics in TypeScript?
options: A) A way to make all types optional
B) A way to create reusable components that work with a variety of types rather than a single one
C) A shorthand for union types
D) A way to convert types at runtime

questions.question: What does `T` represent in `function identity<T>(arg: T): T`?
options: A) The string 'T'
B) A type parameter that is determined when the function is called
C) The type 'any'
D) A class named T
A) undefined
B) 'hello' with type string
C) 'hello' with type any
D) Compile error

questions.question: Can TypeScript infer generic type arguments?
options: A) No, you must always specify them explicitly
B) Yes, TypeScript can often infer the type from the arguments passed
C) Only for primitive types
D) Only when using arrow functions
A) A way to limit T to types that have a specific shape or property
B) A way to make T optional
C) A way to set T to a default type
D) A way to make the function accept only strings
A) getLength('hello')
B) getLength([1, 2, 3])
C) getLength(42)
D) getLength({ length: 10, name: 'test' })

questions.question: What does `keyof` do in TypeScript?
options: A) Creates a new key on an object
B) Produces a union type of all property names (keys) of a given type
C) Checks if a key exists at runtime
D) Converts an object to an array of keys
A) any string
B) A union of the property names of T
C) The type of the values in T
D) number
B) 'Alice'
C) undefined
D) Compile error
A) An interface that only works with strings
B) An interface with a type parameter that can be specified when used
C) An interface that generates code at runtime
D) An interface that extends all other interfaces
A) any
B) number
C) string
D) Box<number>
A) A class that only works with one type
B) A class with type parameters that can be specified when instantiated
C) A class that generates multiple classes at runtime
D) An abstract class
A) any
B) string
C) string | undefined
D) unknown
A) A type that is always used regardless of what you pass
B) A fallback type used when no type argument is explicitly provided
C) A type that overrides any provided type argument
D) A type that is only used for error cases

questions.question: What is the type of `data` in `const r: Response = { data: 'ok' };` given `interface Response<T = string> { data: T; }`?
options: A) any
B) unknown
C) string
D) 'ok'
A) No error
B) Compile error: Type 'number' is not assignable to type 'string'
C) Runtime error
D) Returns undefined
A) No, generics only support one type parameter
B) Yes, you can have any number of type parameters
C) Only up to two
D) Only in interfaces, not functions

questions.question: What does this constraint mean: `<T extends string | number>`?
options: A) T must be exactly string or number
B) T must be a subtype of string or a subtype of number
C) T is always a string
D) T cannot be a string or number
A) { wrapped: any }
B) { wrapped: boolean }
C) { wrapped: true }
D) boolean

questions.question: Why use generics instead of `any`?
options: A) Generics are faster at runtime
B) Generics preserve type information and provide type safety; `any` discards it
C) `any` is deprecated in TypeScript
D) There is no advantage; they are equivalent

questions.question: What does `T[K]` mean in TypeScript (indexed access type)?
options: A) The Kth element of array T
B) The type of the property K on type T
C) A new type combining T and K
D) An index signature
A) { x: number }
B) { y: string }
C) { x: number; y: string }
D) any
A) K can be any string
B) K must be one of the actual property names of T
C) K must be a number
D) T must be an array
A) ['Alice', 30]
B) ['Alice', 'NYC']
C) [{ name: 'Alice' }, { city: 'NYC' }]
D) Compile error
A) No, constraints are only for functions
B) Yes, class type parameters can have constraints using extends
C) Only abstract classes can have constrained generics
D) Constraints are not supported in TypeScript
